coredata.dc:title: Tunable dielectric characteristics of the nanocomposite diode based on functionalized graphene quantum dots with and without gadolinium
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Berktaş, Zeynep
Anter, Aslıhan
Dikicioğlu, Elanur
Ulusoy, Murat
Candan, Can
Yıldız, Mustafa
Di Bartolomeo, Antonio
Orhan, Elif
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we have separately synthesized and characterized solutions of gadolinium (Gd)-free and Gd-doped polyethyleneimine (PEI)-functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) due to the excellent properties of Gd, a rare earth element, in fluorescence and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). The dielectric properties of Gd-free/doped nanocomposite-based diodes have been compared using impedance spectroscopy (IS) in the frequency range from 1 kHz to 500 kHz and voltage range from −3 V to +5 V at 300 K. From our experimental results, the Gd-free diode was found to have a negative dielectric constant (ε′). In contrast, the Gd-doped nanocomposite diode exhibited positive ε′. The ε′ of the Gd-free diode is −80, while the ε′ of the Gd-doped diode is 35 at 5 V for 1 kHz. The experimental results showed that the dielectric properties of both structures were strongly dependent on the applied voltage and frequency. The Gd doping in the interface has prevented the domination of loss mechanisms within the structure, eliminated negative dielectric at lower frequencies, and conferred a unique micro-capacitor characteristic to the structure. All these efforts will contribute to the development of functional carbon-based materials and the creation of new electronic devices and tunable dielectric properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
Gadolinium free/doped GQDs nanocomposite
Positive/negative dielectric constants

Fabrication of a GQDs nanocomposite-based Schottky diode
Characterization of Gd-free and doped GQDs nanocomposites
Analysis of dielectric properties of Gd-free and doped GQDs nanocomposites

E.O. is grateful to the Gazi University Scientific Research Council (BAP) for the financial support of this research under project FGA-2022-8252. The authors are grateful to the Gazi University Electro-Optic Research Laboratory. All data included in this study are available upon request by contact with the corresponding author (E.O.).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The schematic representation of GQDs nanocomposite diode, (b) schematic presentation of the measurement system.
XPS spectra of (a) Gd-free GQDs, (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) O1s.
XPS spectra of (a) Gd-doped GQDs nanocomposite, (b) Gd4d (c) N1s, (c) C1s, and (d) O1s.
TEM images of (a) Gd-free and (b) Gd-doped PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposites.
ε′-V profiles of (a) a Gd-free and (b) Gd-doped GQDs diodes, ε′′-V profiles of (c) Gd-free and (d) Gd-doped GQDs diodes.
ε′-ln(f) profiles of (a) Gd-free and (b) Gd-doped GQDs diodes, ε′′-ln(f) profiles of (c) Gd-free and (d) Gd-doped GQDs diodes.
M′ -V profiles of (a) Gd-free and (b) Gd-doped GQDs diodes, M″ -V profiles (c) Gd-free and (d) Gd-doped GQDs diodes.
M′ -ln(f) profiles of (a) Gd-free and (b) Gd-doped GQDs diodes, M″ -ln(f) profiles of (c) Gd-free and (d) Gd-doped GQDs diodes.
Z′-V profiles of (a) Gd-free and (b) Gd-doped GQDS diodes, Z″ -V plots of (c) Gd-free and (d) Gd-doped GQDs diodes.
σ AC –V profiles of (a) Gd-free and (b) Gd-doped GQDs diodes, σ AC -ln(f) profiles of (c) Gd free and (d) Gd-doped GQDs diodes.
tanδ-V profiles of (a) Gd-free and (b) Gd-doped GQDs diodes, tanδ-ln(f) profiles of (c) Gd-free and (d) Gd-doped GQDs diodes.
Phase angle (θ) of (a) Gd-free and (b) Gd-doped GQDs diodes.
Cole–Cole plot of complex modulus profiles of (a) Gd-free and (b) Gd-doped GQDs diodes at typical frequencies of 50 kHz, 70 kHz, 300 kHz, and 500 kHz.

head.ce:title: Tunable dielectric characteristics of the nanocomposite diode based on functionalized graphene quantum dots with and without gadolinium

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Salerno, Department of Physics “E.R. Caianiello”, 84084 Fisciano, Salerno, Italy
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: eUniversity of Salerno, Department of Physics “E.R. Caianiello”, 84084 Fisciano, Salerno, Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we have separately synthesized and characterized solutions of gadolinium (Gd)-free and Gd-doped polyethyleneimine (PEI)-functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) due to the excellent properties of Gd, a rare earth element, in fluorescence and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). The dielectric properties of Gd-free/doped nanocomposite-based diodes have been compared using impedance spectroscopy (IS) in the frequency range from 1 kHz to 500 kHz and voltage range from −3 V to +5 V at 300 K. From our experimental results, the Gd-free diode was found to have a negative dielectric constant (ε′). In contrast, the Gd-doped nanocomposite diode exhibited positive ε′. The ε′ of the Gd-free diode is −80, while the ε′ of the Gd-doped diode is 35 at 5 V for 1 kHz. The experimental results showed that the dielectric properties of both structures were strongly dependent on the applied voltage and frequency. The Gd doping in the interface has prevented the domination of loss mechanisms within the structure, eliminated negative dielectric at lower frequencies, and conferred a unique micro-capacitor characteristic to the structure. All these efforts will contribute to the development of functional carbon-based materials and the creation of new electronic devices and tunable dielectric properties.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The materials’ dielectric properties used in semiconductor device technology play a major role in the performance of integrated circuits. Dielectric materials can store charge or energy under the application of an electric field [1,2] and are exploited in many electronic and power devices. In addition, all high-frequency circuit operation depends on the dielectric properties of the materials, and high-frequency electronic circuits are built using suitable dielectric materials [3]. The dielectric constant ε (epsilon) is the fundamental physical parameter of a dielectric material and determines its response to electricity. The dielectric constant is normally positive, but studies in recent years have shown that ε can also be negative [4,5]. A negative dielectric material, also known as a negative permittivity material, shows a negative permittivity value when an electric field is applied. Materials with negative permittivity are widely used in energy storage, transistors, antennas, wireless communications, etc. [6]. Epsilon-negative materials have unique physical features such as negative permeability, negative refractive index, Cerenkov effect, and inverse Doppler effect [7–9]. Such materials have great interest in many applications such as absorption, communications, aerospace, and electromagnetic wave-shielding [10,11]. It is thought that negative dielectric properties can be created in some hetero-composites, including carbon/metal composites and ceramic composites, and this will advance the development of electromagnetic functional materials, especially metamaterials [12–15]. Therefore, understanding the relationship between material design, composition, and microstructure is crucial to controlling the mechanism of the negative dielectric property, though a comprehensive understanding remains elusive.

This study focuses on carbon materials, particularly graphene quantum dots (GQDs), due to their abundance and unique properties. GQDs exhibit a band gap and photoluminescence effect dependent on size, offering special optoelectronic properties distinct from pure graphene. Doping GQDs with heteroatoms has the potential to enhance their properties for electronic device performance, as well as for energy conversion and storage [16]. Numerous studies have been conducted on the synthesis and applications of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-doped GQDs) [17–19]. N-doped GQDs show potential for modifying their chemical, electronic, and structural characteristics, thereby further improving the exceptional properties of GQDs in energy and environmental applications [20]. Additionally, heteroatom doping, including with lanthanide metal Gd, has garnered interest for its potential to modify GQDs' properties [21,22]. GQDs can be functionalized with carboxyl, amide, nitro, aldehyde, cyano, and isopropyl groups. Nanocomposites of GQDs functionalized with polyethyleneimine (PEI), an amino-group polymer, have been synthesized for electronic, medical, and biological applications [21,23,24]. Doping and functionalization methods, essential for tailoring semiconductor materials, hold promise for optimizing GQDs-based devices [21,25].

The ongoing challenge lies in enhancing the performance and reducing the cost of metal-semiconductor (MS) devices, prompting research into new interfacial materials [26–28]. Interfacial layers profoundly impact the performance of MIS-type structures like photodiodes and solar cells, with their dielectric constant and homogeneity playing crucial roles [29]. Impedance spectroscopy (IS) serves as a valuable tool for studying these devices, revealing insights into conduction mechanisms and electrical parameters [30]. Four types of polarization—electronic, ionic, orientation, and interfacial—affect impedance measurements based on external electric fields and frequencies [31,32]. They are called electronic, ionic, orientation, and interfacial polarizations. While ionic and electronic polarizations may become important only at high frequencies (1010–1015 Hz), orientation and surface polarizations dominate in the low and intermediate frequencies, respectively. The change in the dielectric constant with frequency has been explained by a researcher and co-workers, [33], in terms of relaxation time. The dielectric constant is high at low frequencies because the electric dipoles have time to align with the field. As the dipoles have less time to align, the dielectric constant decreases at high frequencies. Especially at low or intermediate frequencies, the dipoles and surface states can respond to the electric field and reorient under the direction of the external electric field so that orientation and surface polarizations become dominant [31,32].

Considering the above, this study investigates the frequency-voltage-dependent dielectric characteristics of GQDs-based nanocomposite diodes, with and without Gd, using IS in the 1 kHz to 500 kHz frequency range and voltage range from −3 V to +5 V at room temperature. The voltage and frequency-dependent real/imaginary parts of permittivity (ε′, ε″), complex electric-modulus (M′, M″), impedances (Z′ and Z″), tangent-loss (tanδ), and ac electric-conductivity (σ AC) of the Gd free GQDs based structure have been investigated using the C-G/ω-V-f results of our previous study [25,34]. Here, the dielectric properties of the GQDs-based Schottky diode are investigated, unlike in the previous studies [21,25,34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of functionalized Gd-free and doped GQDs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of GQDs functionalized with polyethyleneimine (PEI) (Mw: 1800, 50 wt.% in H2O) and doped with nitrogen was carried out by the hydrothermal method. Starting from citric acid (CA), the hydrothermal reaction was carried out in an autoclave using an aqueous medium. The solution of GQDs was prepared in deionized (DI) water. The details of synthesizing PEI-functionalized GQDs with N doping can be found in our previous work [21,25,34]. After synthesizing, Gd-free functionalized GQDs were obtained. Gd-doped functionalized GQDs were synthesized in addition to this study. A Gd(NO3)3 solution was used for the synthesis of the Gd nanoparticles. Details of how to synthesize Gd-doped functionalized GQDs have been described in our previous work [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of a GQDs nanocomposite-based Schottky diode
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, the p-Si (100) wafers were chemically cleaned. Aluminum (Al) (99.999 % purity) was chosen for both the top and back contacts because of its cheap and easy availability. A 124 nm thick layer of Al was then deposited by physical vapor deposition (PVD). This provided an ohmic contact. To bind Al to the substrate surface, the coated surface was annealed at 500 °C for 10 min in a vacuum. In the second step of the study, the thin films of PEI-functionalized GQDs solutions with Gd and without Gd were coated by the spin-coating method onto the p-type Si wafers separately. In the previous study [21,25,34] detailed information on the diode fabrication was presented. Finally, 128 nm thick, 1 mm dia. Al contacts were deposited by PVD. A typical diode structure is shown in Fig. 1 (a). Impedance measurements were conducted using an HP 4192A LF impedance analyzer that was controllable between 1 kHz and 1 MHz (± 50 ppm) inside the cryostat, both to control the measurements at room temperature and to avoid possible noise effects. The measurements were performed via a microcomputer using an IEEE-488 AC-DC converter card. The pictured measurement system can be seen in Fig. 1(b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Gd-free and doped GQDs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: PEI functionalized Gd-free GQDs were examined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) to analyze their elemental composition and bonding configurations. The spectra of oxygen (O), nitrogen (N), and carbon (C) bond configurations in PEI GQDs are given in Fig. 2 . In the XPS spectrum of Gd-free GQDs, three peaks, C, N, and O, were observed and the percent carbon/nitrogen ratio was about 4.49. As can be seen from Fig. 2(a), the peaks at 284.58, 400.08, and 531.58 eV belong to the binding energy of C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively [35].

When the C1s spectrum of Gd-free GQDs is analyzed, the main peak at 284.58 eV corresponds with sp2-carbon atoms that form graphitic domains, suggesting that most of the C atoms have honeycombed arrangements. Two additional peaks at 285.78 and 287.78 eV are also observed in the spectrum, corresponding to CN and CN bonds. These peaks are evidence of the successful doping of N into GQDs in the hydrothermal reaction (Fig. 2(b)). The peaks observed between 285 and 288 eV in the C1s spectrum of PEI N-doped GQDs originate from side groups in the graphene molecule and correspond to sp3, CO, CN, OCO and CO groups [36]. In the high-resolution N1s spectrum of N-doped PEI GQDs (Fig. 2(c)), three peaks were found at 399.88 (pyridinic-N), 400.08 (pyrrolic-N) and 400.18 eV (graphitic-N). It can be concluded from the spectrum that pyrrolic-N is the main component. Again, in the high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 2(d)), the three peaks observed at 531.18, 531.48, and 531.78 eV are attributed to the presence of different bound oxygen, such as CO, CO, and OCO [37]. According to XPS results, PEI functionalized N-doped Gd-free GQDs were formed, and their structures were as given.

The elemental composition and bonding configurations of Gd nanoparticles-doped GQDs nanocomposite were investigated by XPS. The spectra of C, N, O, and Gd bond configurations in Gd-doped GQDs nanocomposites are as given in Fig. 3 . When Fig. 3 is analyzed, the peaks at 165.08, 298.08, 420.08, and 545.08 eV belong to the binding energy of Gd4d, C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. According to the XPS results, it can be said that Gd-doped GQDs nanocomposites were formed and their structures were as given.

The morphologies of PEI N-doped GQDs and GdNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposites were evaluated by TEM analysis (Fig. 4 (a) and (b)). As shown in Fig. 4a, the PEI N-doped GQDs are spherical and have particle sizes ranging from 2 to 9 nm and show no agglomeration. When the TEM image of GdNPs/PEI N-doped GQDs nanocomposites in Fig. 4(b) is examined, it is seen that the formed structures are spherical with sizes ranging from 2 to 14 nm and do not exhibit any agglomeration. As can be seen from Fig. 4(b), PEI N-doped GQDs are smaller spherical structures and GdNPs are larger and randomly distributed among PEI N-doped GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of dielectric properties of Gd-free and doped GQDs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para:
Dielectric properties are useful in determining possible applications of the material. The response of a structure to an applied electric field is determined by its permittivity and conductivity. Complex permittivity (ε*) consists of dielectric permittivity (ε'; real part) and dielectric loss (ε″; imaginary part). ε' corresponds to the ability of the material to store energy, while ε″ corresponds to the ability to dissipate energy in the form of heat due to the friction between the dipoles. ε′ and ε″ values were extracted from dielectric impedance spectroscopy over a broad range of frequencies and voltages, providing further insight into polarization mechanisms and their behavior in depleted and enriched regions. The values of ε′ and ε″ values of Gd-free/doped functionalized GQDs nanocomposites were extracted from the C-G/ω data in our previous work [25,34] using Eq. 1
(1)
ε
*
( ω )
=
ε
′
( ω )
− j
ε
″
( ω )
=
C
C o
− j
G
ω
C o
In Eq. 1, Co is the capacitance of the unfilled capacitor (=εoA/di), A is the area of the contact, and di is the thickness of the thin film of functionalized GQDs with and without Gd.

Fig. 5 (a) and (b) show the voltage-dependent ε′-V profiles of Gd-free and Gd-doped GQDs nanocomposite diodes in the 1 kHz to 500 kHz frequency range, respectively. The voltage-dependent ε″-V profiles of Gd-free and Gd-doped GQDs nanocomposite diodes are shown in Fig. 5(c) and (d), respectively.

To observe the variations of the ɛ′ and ɛ″ with frequency, graphs of ε′ and ε″-ln(f) were drawn in 50 mV steps between 3.1 V and 4.9 V. Fig. 6 (a) and (b) show the frequency-dependent profiles ε′-ln(f) of Gd-free and Gd-doped GQDs diodes. The frequency-dependent ε″-ln(f) profiles of Gd-free and Gd-doped GQDs nanocomposite diodes are shown in Fig. 6(c) and (d), respectively.

As shown in Fig. 5., both ε″ and ε′ values are highly dependent on frequency and applied bias voltage. From Fig. 5(a), it can be seen that ε′ increases with increasing voltage up to about 3 V. The embedded graph in Fig 5(a) shows that ε′ increases with voltage up to about 3 V, then the curve turns and reaches negative values at low frequencies 1–10 kHz, thus confirming a negative dielectric (ND) behavior [38–42]. Due to some polarisations such as electronic/ionic/dipolar polarisation, the dielectric constant increases at low frequencies [43]. As the frequency increases, the polarisation disappears, reaching a constant state. In addition, while the profile of ε′ goes negative, ε″ profiles approach the maximum (Fig. 5(c)) for the Gd-free structure. Fig. 5(b) shows ε′ profiles of the Gd-doped structure. When doped with Gd, the structure has a positive dielectric constant, and ε′-V values increase with a decrease in frequency. ε″ -V values decrease as frequency increases (Fig. 5(d)). Such behavior of ε′ and ε″ has been attributed to the presence of trap levels or surface states and series resistance (Rs ). These states, originating from diverse sources, strongly affect dielectric characteristics based on the frequency and voltage (field) applied to the structure, particularly during impedance measurements. At relatively low frequencies, the ability to pursue the AC signal in relation to the lifetimes (τ) of the traps/states at the trap level in which they reside often significantly affects the parameter changes in the depletion zone. In addition, changes corresponding to specific voltage regions or energy levels can be observed in ε′ and/or ε″ as the voltage fluctuation in the contact region moves the energy levels of the traps along with the Fermi level up and down. The dielectric values (see Figs. 5 and 6) and related parameters (M′, M″, Z′, Z″, etc.) are influenced by the charge/discharge states of the charges within the space charge region. This influence is determined by the polarization capabilities of the trapped charges at different frequencies and the charge transmission mechanisms supported by the trap levels [44]. This polarization mechanism, referred to as dipole and interfacial or Maxwell-Wagner-Sillars (MWS) polarization, prevails at low frequencies [44].

The change in ɛ' and ε" at lower frequencies can be explained by the fact that τ = 1/2πf, which is higher than the lifetime of the surface states. As ε′ decreases with an increasing positive voltage, ε″ increases.

At lower frequencies, nanocomposite films exhibit high dielectric constant values. As the frequency increases, the dielectric constant decreases, possibly due to the MWS polarization effect or interfacial polarization effect, wherein charge carriers accumulate at the interface [45–47]. Especially at low frequencies, the maximum value of ε″ is almost equal to the minimum value of ε′ in a Gd-free structure. This type of response is also referred to in the literature as the 'inductive response' [44]. Due to relaxation phenomena, the dielectric loss decreases as frequency increases. From Fig. 5(a), it can be seen that the negative behavior starts to disappear at 10 kHz. It should be noted that the observation of the ND behavior in the ε′-V plot or the inductive behavior in a positive bias range is an important result. Although numerous studies aim to elucidate the phenomenon of NC or ND, its nature remains the focus of attention. The generally accepted view is that this behavior is linked to a loss mechanism or recombination process resulting from either high levels of minority carrier injection or high-energy trap charges colliding with concentrated charges below the Fermi level, depending on the applied field. At sufficiently high bias voltage, the effect of recombination may exceed that of diffusion, and then C or ε′(=C/C 0) begins to decrease, as suggested by Zhu et al. [48]. Electrical relaxation in MOSFETs is known to be caused by interface effects, phase changes, polarizing processes, and conduction mechanisms, especially at low and medium frequencies. Gd doping into the interface has prevented the domination of loss mechanisms within the structure, eliminated ND at lower frequencies, and conferred a unique micro-capacitor characteristic to the structure. Moreover, the rapid ε′ and/or ε″ changes during the transition from 50 kHz to 70 kHz indeed demonstrate the polarization and functional differences between the two structures. Clarification of the phenomenon is necessary for further understanding and development in this field.

Probing dielectric properties can be furthered with the complex electrical modulus M*(ω), which allows the investigation of charge conduction mechanisms and polarization processes. Similar variations in the M*(ω) are observed for both ε′ and ε″ components of ε* at low frequencies. The M*(ω) values are the inverse of the complex dielectric, ε*(ω), and correspond to the dielectric relaxation process [44]:
(3)
M
*
( ω )
=
1
ε
*
( ω )
=
M ′
( ω )
+ j
M
″
( ω )
=
ε
′
ε
′
2
+
ε
″
2
+ j
ε
"
ε
′
2
+
ε
″
2

Using the obtained experimental ε′ and ε′′ values for each frequency and applied bias voltage, the M′ and M′′ values in Eq. (3) were calculated. The magnitude of M′ and M′′ corresponds to electronic charge or energy storage capability and is also related to the amount of energy loss. Fig. 7 (a) and (b) show the M′ -V plots for each frequency for Gd-free and Gd-doped structures. M″ -V plots for each frequency for Gd-free and Gd-doped structures are shown in Fig. 7(c) and (d). The M′ -V plots for Gd-free structure increase with increasing frequency, decrease with the bias voltage, and are almost flat at negative voltage. At 500 kHz, the maximum value is 0.57. The M′ -V plots for Gd-doped structure increase with increasing frequency and have a relaxation peak. With increasing frequency, each peak shifts from 0.45 V to −0.05 V towards the negative. The peak has a maximum value (∼33) at 500 kHz. M″ - V plots for Gd-free structure increase with increasing frequency, decrease with the bias voltage, and are almost flat at negative voltage. The maximum value is 0.29 at 500 kHz. Especially, at low frequencies M″ -V plots have different behaviors. M″ -V plots for Gd-doped structure give a peak at high frequencies. Each peak shifts to the negative voltage from 1 V to −0.85 V with decreasing frequency. The maximum values of peaks decrease with increasing frequency. In addition, the splitting of the peaks at 70 kHz, 300 kHz, and 500 kHz was also observed. All peaks at 70 kHz, 300 kHz, and 500 kHz reach their minimum values at −0.05 V. The observed peaks alongside the voltage and frequency values, as well as the peak position shifts, derive from the particular trap/state distributions and their repositioning and reorientation under the field, which correlate with their lifetime [44]. Especially in the Gd-doped structure, there are significant variations in the values of M' and M'' after 50 kHz, indicating an amplified frequency sensitivity of the Gd composite interface.

To consider from a different angle, M′-ln(f) plots are shown in Fig. 8 (a) and (b) for Gd-free and Gd-doped GQDs structures, respectively, for different applied bias voltages. M″-ln(f) plots are represented in Fig. 7(c) and (d). Fig. 8(b) and (d) show that M′ and M′′ are considerably changing with the frequency, especially at moderate frequencies. Both M′ and M′′ are closer to zero at low frequencies. However, they increase at moderate frequencies and finally reach maximum values for each bias voltage due to the interface and dipole polarization. In addition, both the M′-ln(f) and M″-ln(f) of Gd-free GQDs show an intersection point at about 300 kHz and 50 kHz respectively. This is due to the conductive behavior of ε′-V and ε″-V plots at high bias voltage. This is due to the fact that there is not enough free charge at low frequencies. M′-ln(f) and M″-ln(f) profiles of Gd-doped GQDs structure show a high intersection point at about 70 kHz. The increases in modulus for both structures at values ranging from low frequencies to high frequencies are due to the mobility range - either short or long - of the charge carriers and their dielectric relaxation durations (ωτ = 1). The interfacial polarization mechanism, which is influenced by the polarization of charges and relaxation times, can lead to significant results under specific circumstances. The Gd-doped structure's modulus values show sudden drops and even negative shifts beyond 50 kHz, indicating that the frequency-dependent phase shifts stem from the effect of Gd within the mechanism.

The complex impedance (Z* = Z′ + jZ′′) allows us to recognize contributions by the greatest resistance as well as electrical modulus [46].
(4)
Z ′
=
ε
″
[
ω
(
ε
′
2

2
)
C 0
]
Z
″
= −
ε
′

ε
′
2
+
ε
″
2
)
C 0
]

Z′-V and Z″ -V plots for Gd-free and Gd-doped structures at different frequencies are given in Fig. 9 . With increasing frequency, the value of Z' decreases. This can be explained by considering that the hopping of electrons increases with increasing frequency, and therefore Z′ and Z″ values decrease. Z′ graphs for the Gd-free structure show a straight line in Fig. 9(a). As charges accumulate on the high-density side, space charge polarization occurs at low frequencies. This increases the value of Z' [44–52]. When doped with Gd, Z′-V and Z″- V have peaks in Fig. 9(b) and (d). It shows that there is a distinct peak at 70 kHz. This is due to a particular distribution of surface states and dislocations and their restructuring/rearrangement under an electric field [44–52]. The peak was split around −0.05 V. Each peak in the plots of Z′-V and Z″ -V was shifted towards the positive voltage.

The AC conductivity, σ AC can be extracted from the obtained ε″ or tanδ by the following relation [44–52]:
(4a)
σ
A C
=
(
d i
A
)
ω C
t a n δ =
ε
″
ω
ε
0

Furthermore, the power law can be used to define σ ac as [44–46]:
(4b)
σ
A C
=
σ
D C
+ A
ω
n
where σ DC and ω (=2πf) are the DC component of σ AC and the angular frequency, respectively. A and n are the constant and the dimensionless frequency exponent and give important information on the conduction processes of the functionalized GQDs device. The magnitude of n can be obtained from the slope of the linear regime of the double logarithmic σ DC -ω plot. Both the σ AC -V are obtained from Eq. 4(a) and are shown in Fig. 10 (a-b) for Gd-free and Gd-doped structures, respectively. σ AC -ln(f) curves were shown in Fig. 11 (c-d) for Gd-free and Gd-doped structures, respectively. The AC conductivity of Gd-free GQDs (Fig. 10(a)) is almost frequency-independent at low frequencies and then increases almost exponentially for different bias voltages at higher frequencies. Linear segments with different intercepts and slopes for the Gd-free GQD structure are shown in Fig. 10(c). Fig. 10(b) shows σ AC -V plots for Gd-doped GQDs structure. It is interesting to note that at 70 kHz, 300 kHz, and 500 kHz, the AC conductivity is the same and minimal.

It is important to highlight the Gd effect once again, as there is a significant and rapid decline in σ AC for both the forward and reverse regions during the transition from 50 kHz to 70 kHz. This behavior is typical and may be attributed to the mobility charge's hopping mechanisms or the trapping and de-trapping behavior of charges between trap levels. It can be stated that this is a remarkable situation for low-frequency switching applications.

Fig. 11(a) and (b) show tanδ-V profiles of Gd-free and Gd-doped GQDs diodes. tanδ-ln(f) profiles of Gd-free and Gd-doped GQDs diodes are given in Fig. 11(c) and (d). At 1 kHz, the tangent loss has a maximum value (1600) for a Gd-free structure. The tangent loss decreases with increasing frequency. For Gd-doped structure, it is almost independent of frequency up to 70 kHz. Then, it starts to increase almost exponentially at higher frequencies for different applied bias voltages.

As seen in Fig. 11(c), the expected decrease in tanδ with increasing frequency is again attributed to the significant dependence of the changes in ε′ and ε′′ on the distribution of trap/state levels, i.e., energy levels in the forbidden energy range, and also to the presence of Rs . The considerable reduction in tangent loss values observed in the Gd-doped structure, particularly at lower frequencies, in comparison to the undoped structure, can be viewed as a favorable outcome. Apart from this, the crucial point is that while the ε′ and ε′′ changes up to 50 kHz were parallel to each other, the disproportionate and rapid changes of ε′ and ε′′ at 70 kHz and higher frequencies led to surprising peaks in the tanδ values (see Fig. 11(d)). Hereby, whether the interfaces created for such diode structures are doped or non-doped and how the doped nanomaterials will change the electrical characteristics at the interface is an important issue for many researchers.

Phase angle (θ) is the angle between the ohmic current and the capacitive current. The angle alterations of Gd-free and Gd-doped GQDs structures are given in Fig. 12 . At different frequencies, θ changes with the applied voltage. For the Gd-free GQD structure, the phase angle also reaches a minimum value as the frequency decreases. For Gd-doped GQDs structure, each phase angle profile has two distinctive peaks for 70 kHz, 300 kHz, and 500 kHz. The fact that the angle is at its highest value at these frequencies indicates that the pure capacitive response of the structure is around 0 V. The lower value angle peaks and shifts in their positions that subsequently change are due to the individuality of the interface states.

A semicircle, known as the Cole-Cole curve, is obtained by plotting the real part of the modulus of elasticity M* (ω) on the x-axis and its imaginary part on the y-axis. If we plot the real part of the electric modulus on the x-axis and its imaginary part on the y-axis, a semi-circle is obtained, known as the Cole-Cole curve, which represents the primary and single polarization process. Indeed, Fig. 13 (a) shows that M″ vs. M′ for Gd-free GQDs structure has a semicircular profile, which depends on the applied voltage, frequency, and polarization. The radius of this semicircle increases with increasing frequency. This indicates a change in the electrical properties of the devices. This suggests that the prepared sample possesses excellent frequency and voltage sensitivity owing to the presence of interface traps and their relaxation times. Fig. 13(b) shows that M″ vs. M′ for Gd-doped GQDs structure has a semicircular profile. The different behavior was observed at 70 kHz, 300 kHz, and 500 kHz, which go along a circular path and return along a circular path, as shown in embedded Fig. 12(b).

In this study, the frequency-voltage dependent positive/negative dielectric constants of GQDs-based nanocomposite diodes with Gd and without Gd were investigated by impedance spectroscopy in the 1 kHz to 500 kHz frequency range and voltage range from −3 V to +5 V at 300 K by using the C-G/ω-V-f results of our previous study [25,34]. Both Gd-free and Gd-doped, PEI-functionalized GQDs solutions have been separately synthesized by a hydrothermal method and used to form two Schottky diode structures with p-type Si. From our experimental results, the Gd-free diode was found to have a negative dielectric constant-ε′. Currently, the factors affecting the applications of epsilon-negative materials are not well understood. Therefore, there is a need for a more comprehensive understanding of the fundamental physical properties of these epsilon-negative materials. In contrast, the Gd-doped diode exhibited positive ε′. This is an indication of a change in the electrical/dielectric properties of the devices. Gd doping into the interface has prevented the domination of loss mechanisms within the structure, eliminated ND at lower frequencies, and conferred a unique micro-capacitor characteristic to the structure. Moreover, the rapid ε′ and/or ε″ changes during the transition from 50 kHz to 70 kHz indeed demonstrate the polarization and functional differences between the two structures. Clarification of the phenomenon is necessary for further understanding and development in this field. In addition, it is interesting to note that the Gd-doped GQDs nanocomposite device was found to exhibit different behavior, especially at 70 kHz, 300 kHz, and 500 kHz. This suggests that the prepared sample has excellent frequency and applied voltage sensitivity. All these efforts will be helpful for the development of functional carbon-based materials and the creation of new electronic devices.

Zeynep Berktaş: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Aslıhan Anter: Investigation, Formal analysis. Elanur Dikicioğlu: Formal analysis, Data curation. Murat Ulusoy: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation. Can Candan: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Mustafa Yıldız: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Antonio Di Bartolomeo: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Elif Orhan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Investigation, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: E.O. is grateful to the Gazi University Scientific Research Council (BAP) for the financial support of this research under project FGA-2022-8252. The authors are grateful to the Gazi University Electro-Optic Research Laboratory.
All data included in this study are available upon request by contact with the corresponding author (E.O.).
